title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
short_title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
plain_summary: This law tells the Secretary of the Interior to give about 40 acres of land at the Wounded Knee Massacre site to two Native American tribes within one year. The land will become a memorial and sacred site that the Oglala Sioux Tribe and Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe will own together. The tribes cannot sell the land or use it for casinos or other businesses. The land will be protected and follow special rules under federal Indian law.
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 176.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms have specific meanings. Restricted fee status means a special way of owning tribal land where the tribes will continue to own the land. The land will be part of the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation and under the legal control of the Oglala Sioux Tribe. The land cannot be sold or transferred without permission from both Congress and the tribes. State and local governments cannot tax this land. The Secretary of the Interior cannot review or approve how the tribes use the land, as long as they follow the agreement between the Oglala Sioux Tribe and Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe dated October twenty first, two thousand twenty two. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. Tribal land means about forty acres of land on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation in Oglala Lakota County at Rural County Road four in Wounded Knee, South Dakota. This includes everything on the surface, underground, minerals, buildings, and personal property on those acres. This land is shown on a map called Wounded Knee Sacred Site and Memorial Land dated October twenty sixth, two thousand twenty two. This land is part of the place where the Wounded Knee Massacre happened on December twenty ninth, eighteen ninety. Tribes means the Oglala Sioux Tribe and Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe of the Cheyenne River Reservation. Both tribes are part of the Great Sioux Nation and signed the Fort Laramie Treaty in eighteen sixty eight with the United States.

sections.title: Section 3  Land Held in Restricted Fee Status by the Tribes
sections.text: The Secretary must take action within three hundred sixty five days after this law is passed. The Secretary must complete all necessary paperwork and make small corrections to the land survey and legal description so the tribal land can be owned by the tribes in restricted fee status. The Secretary must also properly assign any private and city utility and service rights or agreements for the tribal land. There are conditions for this land. Except for what this law says differently, the tribal land must follow federal laws about Indian country as defined in section eleven fifty one of title eighteen of the United States Code. The land is also protected by the rule against selling it in section one seventy seven of title twenty five of the United States Code. The tribal land can only be used for purposes allowed by the agreement between the Oglala Sioux Tribe and the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe dated October twenty first, two thousand twenty two. The tribal land will still have any private or city restrictions, rights of way, easements on record, or utility service agreements that existed when this law was passed. According to the agreement between the Oglala Sioux Tribe and the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe dated October twenty first, two thousand twenty two, the tribal land cannot be used for gambling activities under the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act.
